speaker: interviewer
text: All quiet, please. We have the tape rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is November 14, 1996. I'm with George Steinberg. And my name is Donna Kenny. We are in Longmeadow, Massachusetts, United States. The interview will be conducted in English. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe you can up.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you, Donna. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] OK, you can start.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good morning, Mr. Steinberg.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to start our conversation by asking you to please state your full name.

speaker: interviewee
text: George Steinberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: And would you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. G-E-R-G-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Steinberg, S-T-E-I-B-E-R-G.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. What was your name when you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: C-E--- no-- G-E-C-E-L-- Gecel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: July the 8th, 1902, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your age today?

speaker: interviewee
text: 92-- 92-- 93-- approximately, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born? What city and country?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a little city-- Wolyn. That's the state, Wolyn. Murecea M-U-R-E-C-E-A-- Wolyn. The state was Wolyn.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please tell me your parents' names.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Enshel-- E-N-S-H-E-L. The last name is Steinberg, like my own name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chaea, C-H-A-E-A-- Steinberg, also-- maiden name, Lerner.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were your parents' occupations?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents-- my-- my pa-- my father was a mason.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he have any other occupations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cattle dealer in wintertime. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Housewife.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We were five brothers and two sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Will you tell me your brothers' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: The brothers' name? The second name is Mendl, M-E-N-D-L. The last name is Steinberg. This-- and-- the third one was a sister. I'll give you her-- it was Faiga-- F-E-G-E-- yeah-- Steinberg. And the-- this is the third one. And the fourth one was a sister again. The name was Bunya. She survived with me, but she's dead. This year she died in Israel-- married and left four children-- brilliant children, all educated-- and find out, because I make it-- fine children. And the husband is still alive, but in Israel. She died. Matter of fact, if you-- I still have a picture what they sent me from her grave now-- over there. And she put them-- the names what I mentioned to you. That was her wish before she died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any other brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: All dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Other brothers-- the first one was Chuna. The last name, Steinberg-- like-- and the other one was Hersh-- and the other one, Yukel. And this was three. And two, we are alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, myself and my brother, which is in-- in Florida.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live when you were growing up as a boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- I was born in the same city from where I gave you-- Mlynow--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell--

speaker: interviewee
text: --Kreis. M-L-Y-N-O-W-- Mlynow. The state was Wolyn-- Kreis Dubno. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: That's what you asked me. That's it. Did other relatives live near you when you were growing up as a boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I had uncles with aunts, but they all killed. Nobody is alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family come together often to--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then when we were alive? Of course, like family-- married, you know-- occasions to visit, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember some of the occasions?

speaker: interviewee
text: I still-- I was younger-- certain occasions, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you celebrate holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We were on that time, more religious, like-- like now, yes, of course. Jewish holidays we're always observing, because this was our custom of life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would your family gather at your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: It depends what kind of occasion. Sometimes it were there. Matter of fact, I had in the same city a brother-in-law. He was a famous man in that time, in that city. And he was picked from four little Jewish [NON-ENGLISH] for-- a head, yes? And one brother-in-law and my-- my sister was-- my wife's sister was married to a rabbi in another city-- all killed, yes. He was a rabbi.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you attend school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, more-- I attended the beginning. My time didn't permit me to be in school. Whatever I learned, I learned for myself and from the world.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do your remember the name of the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: The [NON-ENGLISH]. It was no name. A school-- this was the name for many school. Yeah. It was not only for Jewish. This was for kids in that time. But I didn't-- was lucky enough to catch at that time where to be in school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a favorite teacher?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing what I can mention to you-- I mean, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family attend some--

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I have a-- it came just now to my mind-- a Jewish teacher. The name was Zukor. Yes. It just came to my mind. Certain things comes up. But it's a long time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family attend synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, always. Matter of fact, my-- my father was, in that time, also in the religious-- an officer in the shul. And my father-in-law was even a good cantor. And, you know, on the high holidays, we start to build a synagogue in the little city there to another synagogue. And he was praying for our cantor with no money. Because the shul go for the-- yes. I just-- I remember that, not because I'm making up a story to be big. But this came to my mind, yes. He was a good cantor-- for himself. This was not his profession. They had a flour mill.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name, yes. I can give you the name. Our-- our name was-- there were two synagogues in the city. One name was Olyke-- Olyke like a Jewish shul. And the other was Tresky shul. You know, the names from the shuls, you know. And this was named after big rabbis, chief rabbis over there, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you please spell the name of each synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I can spell you the first-- O-L-Y-K-E was one. And the other one, T-R-E-S-K-Y-- Tresky, yeah. I called, like, you know, the-- it's-- [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit more about your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My father was a decent Jewish man-- [LAUGHS] --with religion, because this was our custom of life for then, yes. And he was in summertime, a mason, and in wintertime, a cattle dealer. Because winter is not masons' work in that time. It's not like over here. Yes, this was occupation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you help your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: If I helped my father-- I did. And-- and this subject, I can answer you-- I mean, the way with the life. I didn't like to be the-- the professions there. I was a young kid, but with a different mind. The mind came for me, naturally, not from the schools or from teachers. I didn't like the way they were-- the professions. But I had to help him when I was younger. I helped him once in a while. And then I became a cattle dealer when I started growing up for myself, Because I didn't like to-- to work. That's what my father told me, because I was-- I didn't want to work physically. I started to work mentally in that time, with my mind-- [LAUGHS] --what I had.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you the oldest in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me about your father's death.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father death-- my father's death was accidentally. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Like I mentioned to you, in the wintertime, he was a cattle dealer. And it's not like we-- here where you have to have a permit. And somebody came and bought from him a little cow. And-- and he sold the cow, but he wants he should kill the cow and give him meat from it. And they called the-- anyway, our law didn't permit to sell. And he called somebody to kill the cow. And he shot the skin. And he cut a little bit with a knife and became poisoned. In one week he died in a big hospital, in the Kreis, the-- in a big city, 14 kilometers from us. He belonged to same city. And a big doctor sent him. And in one week, he died. And he left-- we were already seven children in poor condition, making [NON-ENGLISH] in poor condition. Two little cities was crying of us for the children-- because with no help. And-- and we start to build up by the oldest one-- to build up our lives in that time and that situation. And we succeeded. Financially, after this, because we were all together-- and all together to work-- physically, too. And we succeed financially-- a little bit better than our neighbors or somebody.

speaker: interviewer
text: When your father died--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --who then became head of the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't want to take the credit. My mother was alive, but she couldn't do nothing. She was not a business lady, and we didn't have a business. I was that. I was 16 years old at the time-- 15 or 16 years old. And she believed in God, whatever it is. God gave me common sense. That's my responsibility-- and started to do whatever we could to survive. This-- this was our life.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you help your family survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: To survive? We didn't survive. But when-- what do you mean-- financially? Before the war, we worked physically whatever we could until then-- working-- and we survived somehow. But-- and then, after we became a-- a cattle dealer and we start to succeed financially-- you know, not millionaires, but succeed financially in that time in this type of life.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you meet your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife-- almost-- to tell you, we almost grown up together in the same little city. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] To-- the history from my-- from my wife and my marriage is-- I used to say. But it's not necessary even to mention this thing-- I don't know if it will help. God gave me always common sense. And when I start to go out with my wife, I was a little bit at that time and that area, a little bit financially better off, built up a little bit. And she was the nicest girl at that time, I thought-- to me. And that's the reason what I meet my wife. And we-- we went together before marriage-- maybe eight years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your wife live near you when she was a girl growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was a small city, maybe a 1/2 a mile from myself. It's not a big-- [LAUGHS] difference, I mean.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you or your family experience anti-Semitism when you were growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we were growing up, a lot of the time, when the expression was different, you know, like in Europe-- and-- to tell you my way of thinking, the reason when I would think, is a little-- it's not necessary. The Jew was always-- lived a little bit different than a neighbor, because the area was a-- poor areas. And from where I came-- always, you know, very poor, and always went up. Somebody lives a little bit different-- other-- like in the Jewish [LAUGHS] tradition. They might bake a white bread for Shabbes. The neighbor that didn't have this was jealousy-- nothing special-- because-- you know-- I give you only for example. This was the life in that time, in Europe, and that's all. Because other people-- I never wanted to bring it up-- thing like this-- always a good little effort, because there in the family was the husband. And the Jewish people were not drinkers-- like whisky and other things. Neighbors were different. Every nationality have different ideas, you know. And this was the jealousy-- not other things. [LAUGHS] But nobody else. We were between the both sides-- for this was the jealousy-- little, little different. When Shabbes came, like there were, you know-- a Jew always dressed up and went to the synagogue. And other nationalities didn't do this. This all in the jealousy it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Tell me what you remember of World War I.

speaker: interviewee
text: If I remember the first world war? Matter of fact, I remember certain things, yes. Our city was destroyed when the war started. In that time, nobody-- not our parents, not our friends-- know what a war-- what war mean. And the war came already. Because by us, we had a river. We had-- by-- and it was a place where the war started a little bit harder between the both sides-- the Germans and the-- and the-- and the Poles, it was. Anyway, in that time, this was the jealousy. And our little city was destroyed-- destroyed entirely-- no houses. They were burned up from the war. And we went to a bigger city. The name was Rowno.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. R-O-W-N-O. Yeah. Yeah. We went, because no-- no choice. We came there. While we were already there-- refugees-- enough. In Russian, they called us [RUSSIAN]. And we were living in the synagogue. It was not only myself. And then they took us-- the government-- at that time. The Russian government took us and sent us to Russia. And I was near to Siberia. It was in that time. And the name was Stary Oskol. I can spell you the name from the city. And the state was Kursk. You know-- [LAUGHS] --I still remember the thing. And I was at the time a young kid. And the revolution became there. When they killed the Czar, I was in Russia. And I was a kid, you know, a young kid. And I went with the kids with a flag-- no more Czar-- you know, like any other kids in that time. And this-- yes. What I had was-- God gave me, even from my-- all my experience, from my life, or this-- a little bit of common sense-- not college, not other-- only common sense to survive. And-- and-- that was my-- my life there. And then we came back to our city after. And we started to build a little house with a straw-- the roof-- you know-- just the life, the European life. Yeah, I remember. And in the second world war, I don't have to tell you. I was a survivor by a miracle. And that's because I was smarter than the other that was killed-- a miracle, only.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you explain to me where you were when you went to Russia? Can you spell for me the town you were in in Russia-- Stary Oskol?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can spell it in the way what I can, yeah-- S-T-A-O-O-S-K-L-- by the end, yes. Stary Oskol-- this was the-- the name, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was the state, [NON-ENGLISH], was Kursk-- K-U-R-K-E-A-- at the end. I'm not sure what the spelling, because it's a long time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these areas near any other major cities in Russia?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Russia? Of course, it was-- not too far. And it was not too far even from Moscow. And then it was Belgrade, which I was there. I went there. It's a long story which is not-- it's a long history-- and far. I don't think it was even-- it's necessary and at this-- yes. I remember other states-- cities, too, from Russia, because-- a memory I used to have-- not now. I used to have a good memory-- and without college always tried to manage my life with whatever was necessary in that time-- and after this.

speaker: interviewer
text: You returned to your home in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not to the home. The home was destroyed. I came to a-- to a big city. The name was Lutsk. Why Lutsk? Because my mother-- she'll rest in peace-- used to have there a brother. He used to live there. And that's why we came. And the name was Lutsk-- L-U-C-K-E-- yes, a big city. We went there. And from there, we went back, you know, even-- not that there was-- 34 kilometer from our little city-- from where I came-- from where I was born. And I went with my father, because I was the oldest kid in that time, also-- in the family. And I went with my father the 34 kilometer-- not to-- foot-- with the feet-- 34 kilometer-- and came there. And my father was a smoker. You know, I used to smoke. And when I was a kid, he really permit me. I took from Chuna, leaves, and made also cigarettes from the newspaper to smoke, too. I mean, I came to my mind, the-- the life-- history from my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that you traveled the 24 kilometers with your father. How did you travel that distance?

speaker: interviewee
text: With a horse-- also back. I had already a horse and a wagon at that time. My father had-- but I say, I. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you help your father to build a new home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was the oldest in my family. And whatever that time was not-- I didn't went to school. I went to-- yes-- to have to help him to build a little house, whatever-- with straw was the roof.

speaker: interviewer
text: It had a straw roof?

speaker: interviewee
text: And one more I'll describe again-- describing-- the-- the houses and the-- and the life. This was the life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe for me a little bit what the house was like inside? Was there one room or two rooms?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we had three rooms-- a dining room with a sleeping room and a kitchen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you keep warm in the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the-- in the house? Not to be warm and not to be cold. And my father was a mason. And he built a little-- from-- from-- you know-- bricks-- a little oven. And this was-- and went in wintertime to the-- the other side from the water. And the water was mostly warm. And we cut the-- in that time, they call this-- in that-- I don't even know what they call it. They have little brushes under the top. This, and for this we used to-- to heat up the little house once in a while.

speaker: interviewer
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Hello?

speaker: interviewer
text: There were seven children in the family, and two--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. In that time we were five children only. Two came later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you all sleep in different rooms or--

speaker: interviewee
text: This was not, of course-- how can we have--

speaker: interviewer
text: You all slept in one room?

speaker: interviewee
text: In one room, in two beds.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a barn? Or outside of the house, was there a barn?

speaker: interviewee
text: A small little-- yeah-- yes. Of course, my father had already the horse-- and-- for the horse.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- did your family have any other animals?

speaker: interviewee
text: Our house-- we have a horse and a cow-- later-- after-- not when we came in there. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family grow vegetables, or wheat, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we had a little garden. Yes, just yes. This I remember. We were digging with a shovel, and yes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever serve in the Polish or Russian army?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's a long-- a long history, yes. I was not serving. But I don't know why, but God gave me always luck. And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they took from my-- my friends to the army, and they needed-- they didn't call me. It's only by luck, because even when the army was not so organized [LAUGHS] like so organized in the world now, even there. And they didn't call me. And I didn't went. I was ready, because they called my friends. And I have already in a little bag, a piece of bread with a-- a shirt to-- to go. And they didn't call me-- a miracle. And I was not a-- a fanatic to believe in miracles. What happened to me in my life-- miracles. I survived from the ghetto only by a miracle-- not I was smarter than my neighbors or something. A miracle has happened to me. My-- my wife-- she'll rest in peace-- was more believer than I. And she always mentioned God, because she had the appearance of more religious. You know, and this-- God gave me, but he didn't-- he took her away with me. And this is the two like-- like today's truth then in the world. And near mine was this-- God was with her. And in her-- and here luck, because she came from a religious family.

speaker: interviewer
text: You've talked of your wife a couple of times this morning.

speaker: interviewee
text: And lot of times.

speaker: interviewer
text: Will you tell me--

speaker: interviewee
text: And she always, even over here-- I came over here to United States. Maybe it's not necessary, but you didn't ask me. I made a decent life. You know-- start to work like a Jewish man-- not a drinker, not a spender-- build up my life and live decent, you know, better than maybe another neighbor-- what he didn't like I did. And I build up my life. And my wife was more religious. She always belonged to the-- yes-- charitable-- not because I make it up.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is-- what is your wife's name? Can you tell me your wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife-- Paula. We used to call her here, Paula. The name-- the Jewish name was [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Mr. Steinberg. This is the end of the first tape. And we'll start again in a moment.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.
